subject: maths
title: Triangles

nodes.title: Prove triangles similar and apply similarity to solve problems
nodes.brief: Use similarity criteria (AAA/AA, SSS, SAS) to prove two triangles similar, then use the proportional sides / equal angles to find unknown lengths, angles, or prove further results.
nodes.quote: We now take some examples to illustrate the use of these criteria.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the triangles and their vertex correspondence.
Before choosing a criterion, name the two triangles you are comparing and match their vertices: the vertex opposite the largest side of one pairs with the vertex opposite the largest side of the other. For example, in a figure where angle P = angle S and angle Q = angle T, the correspondence is P↔S, Q↔T, R↔U.

PART 2 — Choose and apply the criterion (AA, SSS, or SAS).
AA: two pairs of equal angles suffice (the third follows automatically). SSS: all three ratios of corresponding sides are equal. SAS: one equal included angle and the two sides framing it are proportional. State the similarity as Delta ABC ~ Delta DEF, ordering vertices by correspondence.

PART 3 — State the similarity symbolically with correct correspondence.
Write Delta ABC ~ Delta DEF only when A↔D, B↔E, C↔F; a mis-ordered name corrupts every ratio that follows. After establishing the similarity, read off AB/DE = BC/EF = CA/FD and angle A = angle D, angle B = angle E, angle C = angle F to finish the problem.

PART 4 — Read off equal angles and proportional sides to finish the problem.
Once Delta ABC ~ Delta DEF is written correctly, the ratio chain AB/DE = BC/EF = CA/FD gives any unknown length by cross-multiplication, and the angle equalities supply any unknown angle directly.
keyMoves: Identify the two candidate triangles and their correct vertex correspondence
Choose the applicable criterion (AA/AAA, SSS, SAS) from the available data
State the similarity symbolically with correct correspondence
Read off equal angles / proportional sides to finish the problem
misconceptions: Believing one condition (only angles equal OR only sides proportional) suffices for general polygons
Writing the similarity with the wrong correspondence of vertices

nodes.title: Recognise similar figures (same shape, not necessarily same size)
nodes.brief: State that two figures are similar if they have the same shape but not necessarily the same size, and that all congruent figures are similar but not conversely.
nodes.quote: Two similar figures have the same shape but not necessarily the same size.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Same shape, not necessarily same size.
Two figures are similar if they have the same shape but may differ in size. A photograph enlarged to poster size is similar to the original; a toy car is similar to the real car. The key test: every angle is preserved and every length scales by the same factor.

PART 2 — Congruent figures are a special case of similar.
Congruent figures have equal shape AND equal size, so the scale factor is 1. All congruent figures are therefore similar, but two similar figures need not be congruent. For example, any two circles are always similar; two circles are congruent only when their radii are equal.
keyMoves: Check whether two figures have the same shape
Recognise congruent figures are a special case of similar
misconceptions: Thinking similar figures must also be the same size
Thinking similar figures must be congruent

nodes.title: State the two conditions for two polygons to be similar
nodes.brief: Two polygons of the same number of sides are similar iff (i) corresponding angles are equal AND (ii) corresponding sides are in the same ratio.
nodes.quote: Two polygons of the same number of sides are similar, if (i) their corresponding angles are equal and (ii) their corresponding sides are in the same ratio (or proportion).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Condition 1: all corresponding angles must be equal.
Match the vertices in order. Every angle at a vertex of one polygon must equal the angle at the corresponding vertex of the other. For quadrilaterals ABCD and PQRS to be similar, angle A = angle P, angle B = angle Q, angle C = angle R, and angle D = angle S must all hold.

PART 2 — Condition 2: all corresponding sides must be in the same ratio.
All four pairs of corresponding sides must satisfy AB/PQ = BC/QR = CD/RS = DA/SP. Both conditions together are necessary for general polygons; for triangles the chapter proves that either condition alone suffices.
keyMoves: Verify all corresponding angles are equal
Verify all corresponding sides are in the same ratio
misconceptions: Assuming equal corresponding angles alone makes polygons similar (square vs rectangle counterexample)
Assuming proportional sides alone makes polygons similar (square vs rhombus counterexample)

nodes.title: Know that one condition alone is insufficient for polygon similarity
nodes.brief: Recognise via the square/rectangle and square/rhombus counterexamples that neither equal angles alone nor proportional sides alone make two polygons similar.
nodes.quote: Thus, either of the above two conditions (i) and (ii) of similarity of two polygons is not sufficient for them to be similar.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Equal angles alone do not guarantee similarity.
A square and a rectangle both have all four angles equal to 90 degrees, but their side ratios differ (a 2×3 rectangle has ratio 2:3 while a unit square has ratio 1:1), so they are not similar. Equal angles are necessary but not sufficient.

PART 2 — Proportional sides alone do not guarantee similarity.
A square and a rhombus can have sides in the ratio 1:1, but a rhombus has angles other than 90 degrees while the square has all 90 degrees, so they are not similar. Both conditions — equal angles AND proportional sides — must be verified before declaring two polygons similar.
keyMoves: Test BOTH conditions before concluding similarity for general polygons
Use a counterexample to reject a one-condition claim
misconceptions: Declaring two polygons similar after checking only angles or only sides

nodes.title: Identify corresponding vertices, angles and sides between two figures
nodes.brief: Match up vertices \(A' \leftrightarrow A, B' \leftrightarrow B,\) etc. and then the corresponding angles and corresponding sides so ratios and equalities are written between matching parts.
nodes.quote: vertex A′ corresponds to vertex A, vertex B′ corresponds to vertex B, vertex C′ corresponds to vertex C and vertex D′ corresponds to vertex D
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — List the vertex-to-vertex correspondence.
Name the pairing explicitly: A↔P, B↔Q, C↔R. The correspondence is usually implied by the order in a similarity statement (Delta ABC ~ Delta PQR means A pairs with P) or by equal angles identifying the matching vertex.

PART 2 — Pair sides and angles from the correspondence.
Once vertices are matched, corresponding sides are the ones joining matched vertex pairs: AB corresponds to PQ, BC to QR, CA to RP. Corresponding angles sit at matched vertices: angle A corresponds to angle P. Writing ratios or equalities between non-corresponding parts is the most common error in similarity problems.
keyMoves: List the vertex-to-vertex correspondence
Pair corresponding sides and corresponding angles from that correspondence
misconceptions: Pairing sides/angles that are not actually corresponding

nodes.title: Write a triangle similarity statement with correct vertex correspondence
nodes.brief: Use the ~ symbol and order the vertices so that corresponding vertices match (Δ ABC ~ Δ DEF means A↔D, B↔E, C↔F).
nodes.quote: we cannot write Δ ABC ~ Δ EDF or Δ ABC ~ Δ FED. However, we can write Δ BAC ~ Δ EDF.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — The '~' symbol encodes the vertex correspondence.
Delta ABC ~ Delta DEF means the first-listed vertex of one triangle corresponds to the first-listed vertex of the other: A↔D, B↔E, C↔F. This determines which sides and angles are 'corresponding' for all later ratio and equality steps.

PART 2 — Order the vertices correctly; any rearrangement that preserves matching is valid.
If the true correspondence is A↔E, B↔D, C↔F, then Delta ABC ~ Delta EDF is correct. Delta ABC ~ Delta DEF with that same data is wrong even though the same two triangles are involved. For example, if angle B = angle D and angle A = angle E, write Delta BAC ~ Delta DEF (matching B first to D, A to E, C to F).
keyMoves: Use '~' for 'is similar to'
Order vertices so matching vertices line up in both names
misconceptions: Writing the second triangle's vertices in any order rather than matched order

nodes.title: Apply the Basic Proportionality (Thales) Theorem to set up AD/DB = AE/EC
nodes.brief: When a line is parallel to one side of a triangle and meets the other two sides, conclude the two sides are divided in the same ratio.
nodes.quote: Theorem 6.1 : If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle to intersect the other two sides in distinct points, the other two sides are divided in the same ratio.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Spot the parallel line cutting the two sides.
In triangle ABC, if line DE is drawn parallel to BC and meets AB at D and AC at E, the theorem guarantees that D and E divide the two sides in the same ratio: AD/DB = AE/EC. The trigger is always 'a line parallel to one side intersects the other two sides'.

PART 2 — Write the equal-ratio equation immediately.
Once DE || BC is confirmed, write AD/DB = AE/EC. This can be rearranged: adding 1 to both sides gives AB/DB = AC/EC (part-to-whole forms). For example, if AD = 3, DB = 5, AE = x and EC = 10, then 3/5 = x/10 gives x = 6.
keyMoves: Spot a line parallel to one side cutting the other two
Write the equal ratio of the two divided sides
misconceptions: Writing AD/AB = AE/EC instead of AD/DB = AE/EC
Applying BPT when the line is not parallel to a side

nodes.title: Prove BPT using the equal-area argument
nodes.brief: Join BE, CD, drop perpendiculars, express area ratios as AD/DB and AE/EC, and use that ar(BDE)=ar(DEC) (same base, same parallels) to conclude AD/DB = AE/EC.
nodes.quote: Note that Δ BDE and DEC are on the same base DE and between the same parallels BC and DE.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Express area ratios using the two perpendicular heights.
Join BE and CD. In triangle ADE, ar(ADE)/ar(BDE) = (1/2 * DE * h1)/(1/2 * DE * h2) where h1 = height from A and h2 = height from B to DE, so ar(ADE)/ar(BDE) = AD/DB. Similarly ar(ADE)/ar(DEC) = AE/EC. This gives two separate ratio equations both equal to ar(ADE).

PART 2 — Use equal areas of same-base same-parallels triangles to combine.
Triangles BDE and DEC share base DE and lie between the same parallels BC and DE, so ar(BDE) = ar(DEC). Substituting: ar(ADE)/ar(BDE) = ar(ADE)/ar(DEC), which means AD/DB = AE/EC — the BPT result.

PART 3 — Conclude with the BPT statement.
The equal-area fact is the pivot: it replaces one denominator with the other, linking the two ratio equations into a single proportion. Without recognising ar(BDE) = ar(DEC), the proof has no way to connect AD/DB to AE/EC.
keyMoves: Express triangle areas as ½ base × height
Form the two ratio equations (1) and (2)
Use equal-area of same-base-same-parallels triangles to combine them
misconceptions: Not recognising ar(BDE) = ar(DEC)
Mixing up which base/height pairs give which ratio

nodes.title: Apply the converse of BPT to conclude a line is parallel to a side
nodes.brief: If a line divides two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, conclude that the line is parallel to the third side.
nodes.quote: Theorem 6.2 : If a line divides any two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then the line is parallel to the third side.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute the two division ratios.
Given a line PQ cutting two sides of a triangle at S and T, compute PS/SQ and PT/TR from the given lengths. For example, if PS = 4, SQ = 2, PT = 6, TR = 3, then PS/SQ = 2 and PT/TR = 2.

PART 2 — Conclude parallelism if the ratios are equal.
If PS/SQ = PT/TR, Theorem 6.2 (Converse BPT) guarantees ST || QR. If the ratios differ, ST is not parallel to QR. This is the reverse of BPT: equal ratios imply parallelism, rather than parallelism implying equal ratios.
keyMoves: Compute the two division ratios
If equal, declare the line parallel to the third side
misconceptions: Confusing the converse with the direct theorem
Comparing the wrong pair of ratios

nodes.title: Decide whether a line is parallel to a side by comparing computed ratios
nodes.brief: Given numeric segment lengths, compute PE/EQ and PF/FR; if equal the segment is parallel to the third side, otherwise it is not.
nodes.quote: For each of the following cases, state whether EF ‖ QR :
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Compute PE/EQ and PF/FR as decimals or fractions.
Substitute the given numeric lengths. For example if PE = 3.9, EQ = 3, PF = 4, FR = 4, then PE/EQ = 3.9/3 = 1.3 and PF/FR = 4/4 = 1. Because 1.3 ≠ 1 the segment is not parallel to QR.

PART 2 — Compare and state the conclusion.
If both ratios simplify to the same value, conclude EF || QR by the converse of BPT. If they differ, conclude EF is not parallel to QR. Always show both ratio computations and state which case applies; omitting the comparison step loses marks.
keyMoves: Compute PE/EQ and PF/FR as numbers
Compare; equal ⇒ parallel, unequal ⇒ not parallel
misconceptions: Comparing PE/PQ with PF/PR instead of the part-to-part ratios when that is not intended
Arithmetic slip in dividing the lengths

nodes.title: Transform a ratio equation by inverting and adding 1
nodes.brief: From AD/DB = AE/EC, invert to DB/AD = EC/AE, add 1 to both sides to get AB/AD = AC/AE, hence AD/AB = AE/AC.
nodes.quote: or, DB/AD + 1 = EC/AE + 1
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Invert both sides of the proportion.
Starting from AD/DB = AE/EC, take reciprocals of both sides to get DB/AD = EC/AE. This is valid because if two fractions are equal their reciprocals are equal.

PART 2 — Add 1 to both sides and recombine.
Add 1: DB/AD + 1 = EC/AE + 1, giving (DB + AD)/AD = (EC + AE)/AE. Since DB + AD = AB and EC + AE = AC, this becomes AB/AD = AC/AE, or equivalently AD/AB = AE/AC. For example, if AD = 3, DB = 5, AE = 6, EC = 10: DB/AD = 5/3, adding 1: (5+3)/3 = 8/3, so AB/AD = 8/3 and AD/AB = 3/8.
keyMoves: Invert both sides of the proportion
Add 1 to both sides
Recombine the sum into a single whole-segment fraction
misconceptions: Adding 1 to only one side
Forgetting DB + AD = AB when recombining

nodes.title: Apply the AAA / AA similarity criterion
nodes.brief: If corresponding angles of two triangles are equal (or just two angles each), conclude the triangles are similar.
nodes.quote: Theorem 6.3 : If in two triangles, corresponding angles are equal, then their corresponding sides are in the same ratio (or proportion) and hence the two triangles are similar.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Find two pairs of equal corresponding angles.
Source the angle equalities from the figure: alternate angles when lines are parallel, vertically opposite angles at a crossing, or a common angle shared by both triangles. In Example 4, angle P = angle S (alternate angles, PQ || RS) and angle POQ = angle SOR (vertically opposite), giving two pairs.

PART 2 — Invoke AA to declare similarity.
Two equal angle pairs suffice: the angle-sum property forces the third pair equal automatically (180° - angle1 - angle2 is the same in both triangles). Write Delta PQO ~ Delta SRO (matched by correspondence), then the theorem guarantees corresponding sides are proportional.
keyMoves: Find two pairs of equal corresponding angles
Invoke AA (or AAA) to declare similarity
misconceptions: Pairing angles that are not corresponding
Thinking all three angle pairs must be checked when two suffice

nodes.title: Justify equal angles from parallel lines, vertically opposite, or common angles
nodes.brief: Produce the equal-angle facts an AA proof needs: alternate angles and corresponding angles from parallels, vertically opposite angles, and a shared/common angle. (Using notation: ∠ P = ∠ S for alternate angles)
nodes.quote: So, ∠ P = ∠ S     (Alternate angles)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Alternate and corresponding angles from parallel lines.
When two lines are cut by a transversal and the lines are parallel, alternate interior angles are equal and corresponding angles are equal. State the parallel lines as the reason: 'angle P = angle S (alternate angles, PQ || RS)'.

PART 2 — Vertically opposite and common angles.
At any intersection, the two pairs of opposite angles are equal — no parallel-line condition is needed. A shared vertex or shared side produces a 'common angle' that equals itself: angle E = angle E. In Example 7, the right-angle condition (lamp-post and girl are vertical) supplies two more equal angles, so AA is complete from right angle + common light-source angle.
keyMoves: Use alternate/corresponding angles when lines are parallel
Use vertically opposite angles at an intersection
Reuse a shared angle as equal to itself
misconceptions: Calling angles 'alternate' when the lines are not parallel
Missing the common angle shared by two triangles

nodes.title: Apply the SSS similarity criterion
nodes.brief: If the three pairs of corresponding sides are in the same ratio, conclude the triangles are similar.
nodes.quote: Theorem 6.4 : If in two triangles, sides of one triangle are proportional to (i.e., in the same ratio of) the sides of the other triangle, then their corresponding angles are equal and hence the two triangles are similar.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Match sides by correspondence (shortest to shortest, etc.).
Order the sides of each triangle from shortest to longest (or use a known correspondence) to pair them correctly. In Example 5: AB = 3.8, BC = 6, CA = 3*sqrt(3) paired with RQ = 7.6, QP = 12, PR = 6*sqrt(3).

PART 2 — Compute all three ratios and confirm equality.
AB/RQ = 3.8/7.6 = 1/2, BC/QP = 6/12 = 1/2, CA/PR = 3*sqrt(3)/(6*sqrt(3)) = 1/2. All three ratios equal 1/2, so by SSS similarity Delta ABC ~ Delta RQP (vertices matched so each ratio pair is AB/RQ, BC/QP, CA/PR).
keyMoves: Order sides by correspondence (smallest-to-smallest etc.)
Form all three side ratios
Confirm they are equal, then declare similarity
misconceptions: Pairing sides incorrectly so the ratios appear unequal
Concluding similarity from only two equal ratios

nodes.title: Compute and compare the ratios of corresponding sides
nodes.brief: Divide each pair of corresponding sides and simplify to check whether all the ratios are equal (e.g. \(\frac{3.8}{7.6} = \frac{1}{2}\)).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Form each corresponding-side fraction and simplify.
Write the ratio as (side of first triangle)/(corresponding side of second triangle). Simplify numerically: 3.8/7.6 = 38/76 = 1/2; 6/12 = 1/2. For surds, cancel the common surd factor first: 3*sqrt(3) / (6*sqrt(3)) = 3/6 = 1/2 (the sqrt(3) cancels).

PART 2 — Check all simplified ratios match.
If AB/DE = BC/EF = CA/FD (all simplify to the same value), the sides are proportional and the SSS or SAS criterion can be applied. If even one ratio differs, the sides are not proportional and the criterion fails for that assignment of correspondence.
keyMoves: Form each corresponding-side fraction
Simplify to lowest terms (including surds)
Check all the simplified ratios match
misconceptions: Arithmetic/simplification errors
Not cancelling common surd factors

nodes.title: Apply the SAS similarity criterion
nodes.brief: If one angle of a triangle equals one angle of another and the sides including those angles are proportional, conclude the triangles are similar.
nodes.quote: Theorem 6.5 : If one angle of a triangle is equal to one angle of the other triangle and the sides including these angles are proportional, then the two triangles are similar.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify the equal included angle.
The equal angle must be sandwiched between the two sides whose ratio you will check. In Example 6, angle AOD = angle COB (vertically opposite angles); the sides including angle AOD in triangle AOD are OA and OD; the sides including angle COB in triangle COB are OC and OB.

PART 2 — Confirm the two including sides are in the same ratio.
Check OA/OC = OD/OB (both sides of angle AOD in numerator, both sides of angle COB in denominator). If so, declare Delta AOD ~ Delta COB by SAS. Note: OA/OC = OD/OB is rearranged from the product OA*OB = OC*OD by dividing both sides by OC*OB.
keyMoves: Identify the equal angle and check it is included between the two sides
Confirm the two including sides are in the same ratio
Declare similarity by SAS
misconceptions: Using a non-included angle with the two sides
Not rearranging a product OA·OB = OC·OD into the correct ratio form

nodes.title: Rearrange an equal-products relation into a side ratio
nodes.brief: From OA · OB = OC · OD derive OA/OC = OD/OB so SAS can be applied.
nodes.quote: So, OA/OC = OD/OB     (1)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Divide both sides of the product equation to isolate the needed ratio.
Given OA * OB = OC * OD, divide both sides by OC * OB: (OA * OB)/(OC * OB) = (OC * OD)/(OC * OB), which simplifies to OA/OC = OD/OB. This is the ratio form needed for SAS (OA and OD are on one side; OC and OB on the other).

PART 2 — Pair the ratio with the sides that frame the equal angle.
In triangle AOD the sides flanking angle AOD are OA and OD. In triangle COB the sides flanking angle COB are OC and OB. The ratio OA/OC = OD/OB pairs exactly those including sides, satisfying SAS. The wrong grouping OA/OB = OC/OD would pair non-including sides and cannot be used.
keyMoves: Divide both sides to isolate the needed ratio
Pair the ratio with the sides including the equal angle
misconceptions: Forming OA/OB = OC/OD (wrong grouping) instead of OA/OC = OD/OB

nodes.title: Read off equal angles and proportional sides from a stated similarity
nodes.brief: Once Δ ABC ~ Δ PQR is established, write AB/PQ = BC/QR = CA/RP and ∠ A = ∠ P, ∠ B = ∠ Q, ∠ C = ∠ R.
nodes.quote: So, AB/PQ = BC/QR = CA/RP     (1)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Use the matched vertex order to write side ratios.
From Delta ABC ~ Delta PQR, read the ratio chain AB/PQ = BC/QR = CA/RP. The rule: take the two vertices that bound a side in one triangle, read the same-position vertices in the second triangle, and form the ratio. AB is bounded by A and B; the matching vertices in Delta PQR are P and Q, giving AB/PQ.

PART 2 — Use the matched vertex order to write angle equalities.
Angle at the first vertex of one triangle equals the angle at the first vertex of the other: angle A = angle P, angle B = angle Q, angle C = angle R. These equalities are direct consequences of the similarity statement and can be used immediately to find unknown angles or to supply angle equality for a further proof.
keyMoves: Use the matched vertex order to write side ratios
Use the matched vertex order to write angle equalities
misconceptions: Reading off ratios/angles using a wrong correspondence

nodes.title: Find an unknown angle using similar triangles and the angle-sum property
nodes.brief: Match the unknown angle to its equal corresponding angle in the similar triangle, then compute it (e.g. via 180° − ∠A − ∠B).
nodes.quote: But ∠ C = 180° – ∠ A – ∠ B     (Angle sum property)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Identify which known angle the unknown corresponds to.
After establishing Delta ABC ~ Delta RQP by SSS, angle P corresponds to angle C (third vertex in each). Given angle A = 80° and angle B = 60°, compute angle C = 180° - 80° - 60° = 40° by the angle-sum property of a triangle.

PART 2 — Transfer the value to the unknown by correspondence.
Since angle P = angle C and angle C = 40°, conclude angle P = 40°. The correspondence step is essential: without knowing which vertex of Delta RQP matches C, you cannot assign the value correctly.
keyMoves: Identify which angle the unknown corresponds to
Compute the known angle by 180° minus the other two
Transfer the value to the unknown by correspondence
misconceptions: Matching the unknown to the wrong corresponding angle
Arithmetic error in the angle sum

nodes.title: Find an unknown length by solving the similarity proportion
nodes.brief: Set up the equal-ratio equation from the similar triangles and solve for the unknown side (cross-multiply and isolate).
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Write the proportion from the similarity.
From Delta ABE ~ Delta CDE (AA), the corresponding sides give BE/DE = AB/CD. Substituting: BE = 4.8 + x (total shadow + girl-to-lamp distance), DE = x (girl shadow), AB = 3.6 m (lamp height), CD = 0.9 m (girl height, converting 90 cm = 0.9 m). The proportion becomes (4.8 + x)/x = 3.6/0.9.

PART 2 — Cross-multiply and solve the linear equation.
Simplify 3.6/0.9 = 4. Cross-multiplying: 4.8 + x = 4x, so 4.8 = 3x, giving x = 1.6 m. Always check units are consistent before substituting; mixing metres and centimetres is the most common error here.
keyMoves: Write the proportion of corresponding sides
Convert units consistently (90 cm = 0.9 m)
Cross-multiply and solve the linear equation
misconceptions: Inverting a ratio when setting up the proportion
Failing to convert cm to m before substituting

nodes.title: Model a real-world height/shadow situation with similar triangles
nodes.brief: Translate a lamp-post-and-shadow or pole-and-tower scenario into two similar right triangles sharing the sun/light angle, then use proportionality.
nodes.quote: So, the shadow of the girl after walking for 4 seconds is 1.6 m long.
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Draw the two right triangles from the scenario.
A vertical lamp-post AB and a vertical girl CD both cast shadows from the same light source E at ground level. Triangle ABE has the lamp-post as one side and its total shadow as the base; triangle CDE has the girl as one side and her shadow as the base. Both triangles share the same light angle at E.

PART 2 — Justify similarity and solve.
Angle AEB = angle CED (same light source, same angle — common angle at E), and angle ABE = angle CDE = 90° (vertical objects), so Delta ABE ~ Delta CDE by AA. Set up AB/CD = BE/DE (corresponding sides) and substitute the given values. For a 3.6 m lamp, 0.9 m girl, 4.8 m distance walked: (4.8 + x)/x = 3.6/0.9 = 4, giving x = 1.6 m.
keyMoves: Draw the two right triangles from the scenario
Justify similarity (shared angle, right angles)
Set the height/shadow proportion and solve
misconceptions: Not realising the two triangles share the light/sun angle
Mismatching which height goes with which shadow

nodes.title: Use medians (and the 2× relation) within similar triangles
nodes.brief: Use AB = 2·AM and PQ = 2·PN to reduce side ratios to median-segment ratios, enabling SAS/SSS on the median sub-triangles.
nodes.quote: But AB = 2 AM and PQ = 2 PN     (As CM and RN are medians)
nodes.explanation:
PART 1 — Replace each full side by twice its median-foot segment.
If CM is the median to AB in triangle ABC, then M is the midpoint of AB, so AM = AB/2, i.e., AB = 2*AM. Similarly, if RN is the median to PQ in triangle PQR, then PN = PQ/2, i.e., PQ = 2*PN. Substituting into AB/PQ = 2*AM / (2*PN) = AM/PN preserves the ratio.

PART 2 — Apply SAS or SSS to the median sub-triangles.
Now triangle AMC and triangle PNR have AM/PN = CA/RP (given) and angle A = angle P (given), so by SAS Delta AMC ~ Delta PNR. Reading off corresponding sides: CM/RN = AC/PR = AB/PQ. This proves the medians are in the same ratio as the sides.
keyMoves: Replace a full side by twice its median-foot segment
Cancel the 2 to keep the same ratio for the half-segments
Apply SAS/SSS to the median sub-triangles
misconceptions: Forgetting the median bisects the side (AM = AB/2)
Not cancelling the factor 2 consistently on both sides

narrative:
## What I read (reviewer pass)

I re-read the full segmented chapter `jemh106.txt` (SEG 0 Introduction; SEG 1–2 Similar Figures; SEG 3 Similarity of Triangles with BPT, its area proof, the converse, and Examples 1–3; SEG 4 Criteria for Similarity with Theorems 6.3–6.5 and Examples 4–8; SEG 5 Summary) and `_maths-exams.txt` (CBSE Standard + Basic SQP and both Marking Schemes). I then independently re-located every node quote and every gate quote in the exact segment it was tagged to, and independently solved every gate from first principles.

## Citation audit (D-120) — every quote re-located verbatim

All 22 node quotes and all 22 gate quotes were confirmed as contiguous, character-for-character substrings of the segment named in their `sec`/`srcKind`:
- Node quotes: define (SEG1 l.16), polygon-conditions (SEG1 l.35), one-insufficient (SEG2 l.71), corresponding-parts (SEG2 l.50), write-symbolically (SEG4 l.295), BPT (SEG3 l.115), prove-BPT (SEG3 l.139), converse-BPT (SEG3 l.171), test-parallel (SEG3 l.257), manipulate-ratio (SEG3 l.195), AAA/AA (SEG4 l.305), source-angles (SEG4 l.397), SSS (SEG4 l.343), compute-ratios (SEG4 l.411), SAS (SEG4 l.375), rearrange-product (SEG4 l.431), read-off (SEG4 l.478), unknown-angle (SEG4 l.419), unknown-length (SEG4 l.459), model-real-world (SEG4 l.467), medians (SEG4 l.482), goal (SEG4 l.389).
- Exam gate quotes: `8. B) 𝛥𝐸𝐴𝐷` (Basic MS l.1080) and `10. B) isosceles and similar` (Standard MS l.338) — both verbatim.

## Fixes applied

1. **`find-unknown-angle-via-similarity` node quote** changed from the two-line `∠ C = ∠ P … But ∠ C = 180° – ∠ A – ∠ B` (the embedded line break risked an exact-substring miss) to the single contiguous line `But ∠ C = 180° – ∠ A – ∠ B     (Angle sum property)` (l.419), which is the actual angle-sum move the node teaches.
2. **`find-unknown-length-via-proportion` gate quote** re-anchored from `i.e., x = 1.6` to the proportion line `(4.8 + x)/x = 3.6/0.9` (l.459) so the quote shows the setup the node is about; answer x=1.6 independently verified (3x=4.8).
3. **`find-unknown-angle` gate quote** re-anchored to the angle-sum line; prompt reworded to name the angle-sum step explicitly; answer 40° verified (180−80−60).
4. **`apply-converse-bpt` / `apply-sss` / `state-polygon-conditions` / `use-medians` prompts** lightly reworded for unambiguity (e.g. 'all three pairs', 'both conditions required', 'cancelling the factor 2'); `use-medians` distractor `AB/PQ inverted` replaced with the concrete `PN/AM` so all options are well-formed ratios.
5. **`identify-corresponding-parts` prompt** spelled out the matched correspondence (B↔E, A↔A, C↔D) so the answer D is deterministic without needing the exam figure; quote unchanged and verbatim.

## Independent answer check

Every numeric gate re-solved: PE/EQ=3.9/3=1.3; 3.8/7.6=0.5; 180−80−60=40; 3x=4.8⇒x=1.6; pole/tower 6/4×28=42. MCQ criterion gates and the two exam-keyed gates (ΔEAD correspondence ⇒ C↔D; chords ⇒ isosceles and similar) all confirmed correct.

## Structure

Single root `prove-and-apply-triangle-similarity`; 22 nodes, 38 edges. Every node appears in ≥1 edge (no orphans); every edge endpoint is a defined slug; graph is acyclic; descent bottoms out at the two bedrock nodes (define-similar-figures, identify-corresponding-parts). 22 gates = 22 nodes (100% coverage).

## What was absent / uncertain

The chapter gives only sketch proofs ('Why?'/'How?') for Theorems 6.2–6.5, so each criterion is modelled as an *application* skill, not a full proof. The area-ratio-of-similar-triangles theorem and Pythagoras are NOT in this edition; RHS appears only as a one-line note. The `identify-corresponding-parts` exam gate is the one place where the prompt reconstructs the SQP figure scenario — the verbatim answer key (ΔEAD) is solid, but the underlying figure is not in the text, so the prompt states the correspondence explicitly to keep the answer deterministic.
absentNotes: Concepts a fuller "Triangles"/similarity treatment would cover but THIS chapter (NCERT 2024-25 jemh106) does NOT teach, so they are intentionally omitted (not failures): (1) The ratio-of-areas-of-similar-triangles theorem (area ratio = square of the side ratio) — entirely absent from this edition. (2) Pythagoras Theorem and its converse — mentioned only in the Introduction as a future application ("apply this knowledge in giving a simple proof of Pythagoras Theorem"), never proved or demonstrated here. (3) RHS Similarity Criterion — appears ONLY as a one-line 'A NOTE TO THE READER', not taught, demonstrated, or exercised, so it is not modelled as a worked skill. (4) Full deductive proofs of Theorems 6.2, 6.3, 6.4, 6.5 — the chapter gives only sketch proofs with '(Why?)'/'(How?)' gaps, so the application of each criterion is modelled rather than its proof. (5) Trigonometric/coordinate methods, mid-point theorem proofs (deferred 'recall from Class IX'), and constructions — not developed in-chapter. (6) Pure exposition omitted as non-failable: similar-figures photography analogy, world-map scale factor, Thales biography, and Activities 1–6 (illustrative, not assessed).
